ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for deep fried milk custard. A0CKP (milk-based dessert) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor with deep-frying preparation noted. Exact code unavailable in current FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Deep Fried Milk is approximately 120 g, consisting of several golden-fried milk custard pieces served as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Milk-based dessert, deep-fried; adapted from closest analogous entries for fried custard and milk-based fried dough products
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Cantonese deep fried milk custard (炸鮮奶). Values were derived by adapting USDA FoodData Central data for fried milk-based custard products and deep-fried batter-coated desserts (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods). The input enriched_json values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against USDA analogues for plausibility. Energy 285 kcal, protein 3.5 g, fat 18.5 g, carbohydrate 26.5 g per 100 g are consistent with USDA data for comparable fried custard products. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g per 100 g based on USDA data for vegetable-oil deep-fried batter products. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture data are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fried Batter-Coated Starch-Based Desserts — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried dough and cornstarch-thickened milk desserts
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese deep fried milk (炸鮮奶) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cornstarch-thickened puddings (GI ~65–67) and fried batter-coated starch products. The fat content from deep-frying and milk protein moderately attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 26.5 × 1.2 / 100) ≈ (65 × 15.84) / 100 ≈ 10.3 g per 120 g serving. This is an estimated value; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (45 mg per 100 g); however, high caloric density and saturated fat from deep-frying warrant moderation
hypertension.guidance: Deep Fried Milk is low in sodium (45 mg/100 g) and does not directly elevate blood pressure through sodium load, but its high fat content and caloric density mean it should be consumed only occasionally by individuals managing hypertension, particularly if palm oil or lard is used for frying.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) combined with refined cornstarch, wheat flour batter, and added sugar results in a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Deep Fried Milk due to its medium GI (65), refined starch and sugar content, and high caloric density from deep-frying, all of which can impair postprandial blood glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.5 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat of 4.5 g/100 g from deep-frying in vegetable or animal oil, combined with high caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep Fried Milk should be consumed only rarely by individuals with cardiovascular disease, as its high total fat and saturated fat content from deep-frying can adversely affect LDL cholesterol and overall cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep Fried Milk (炸鮮奶, Zhà xiānnǎi) is a classic Cantonese dessert originating from the Shunde district of Guangdong province, China. It is prepared by first making a thick milk custard from whole milk, cornstarch, sugar, and sometimes egg white or vanilla, which is cooked until set, then chilled until firm. The firm custard is cut into rectangular blocks or cubes, coated in a light wheat-flour batter, and deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is golden and shatteringly crisp. The result is a striking textural contrast: a crispy, airy outer shell encasing a warm, silky, molten milk center. Per 100 g, Deep Fried Milk provides approximately 285 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 18.5 g fat, and 26.5 g carbohydrate, with 9.5 g sugar and only 45 mg sodium. It is a calorie-dense, moderate-GI (GI ~65) dessert that is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease due to its refined starch, sugar, and deep-frying fat content. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains milk, gluten, and egg allergens. Culturally, it is served as a special-occasion dessert, banquet treat, or restaurant indulgence, and is considered a technically demanding dish requiring precise temperature control during frying.
common_user_queries: How is Zha Xian Nai made?
Is deep fried milk suitable for diabetics?
Deep fried milk GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Deep Fried Milk (炸鮮奶) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central analogues for fried milk-based custard and batter-coated fried starch desserts. The input ai_generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked for plausibility against USDA analogues and retained where consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat is estimated. Detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and water content are unavailable. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for deep fried milk custard are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of April 2026. No approved source explicitly reports these values for this specific Cantonese preparation. 2. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried batter-coated starch products; direct measurement is recommended. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cornstarch-thickened milk desserts and fried batter products; direct GI measurement for 炸鮮奶 has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. The input source cited (Guangdong Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention, http://cdcp.gd.gov.cn) is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2 and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. 5. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical 120 g entries) was collapsed to a single 120 g serving. 6. This food is not a Singaporean dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null.
